topic: Area of Study 6: Contemporary Traditional Music
subject: music

items.q: Does traditional music stay fixed and unchanged over time?
items.correct: No - oral transmission produces constant variation, and contemporary artists keep reworking traditional material in new settings.
items.misconception: Traditional music is frozen in the past and must always be played the same way.
items.random_wrong: Traditional music changes only when governments update it.
items.why: Because folk repertoire lives in performance rather than in fixed scores, every generation reshapes it; 'contemporary traditional music' exists precisely because traditions evolve.

items.q: What is a drone in traditional music?
items.correct: A continuously sustained pitch (or pitches) that accompanies the melody, as on bagpipes or the tanpura.
items.misconception: A drone is a type of slow melody played in the low register.
items.random_wrong: A drone is the fastest dance section of a folk set.
items.why: Students confuse register with function: the drone is an accompaniment device defined by being sustained and unchanging, against which the melody's modal colours stand out.

items.q: How does the Dorian mode differ from the natural minor scale on the same tonic?
items.correct: Dorian has a raised sixth degree compared with the natural minor; otherwise the two scales match.
items.misconception: Modes are just alternative names for the ordinary minor scale.
items.random_wrong: Dorian has no seventh degree at all.
items.why: Each mode has its own interval pattern. Hearing the raised sixth is the key to recognising the Dorian flavour common in folk melody, so collapsing all modes into 'minor' loses the distinction.

items.q: A sustained pitch held underneath a melody is called a what?
items.why: It's Drone. “Riff” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Astor Piazzolla's 'tango nuevo' fused Argentine tango with elements of which traditions?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Jazz and Western classical music. “Gamelan and gagaku” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Music passed on by ear from performer to performer is said to belong to what?
items.why: Correct answer: The oral tradition. “The figured bass tradition” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A folk melody with a major third but a flattened seventh is most likely in which mode?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Mixolydian. “Dorian” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many strings does the West African kora traditionally have?
items.why: Exact answer: 21 strings. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
